sentences.text: [INAUDIBLE] pulled me out of the-- of the grave.
And on a signal, we quit-- you know, another episode I saw, which was very unusual for me, or I didn't expect it-- a truck came, and-- a German truck.
And there were Russian soldiers killed.
All of them were killed here.
There was blood on the back of the-- of the neck.
There was a prisoner camp, a Russian prisoner camp in-- by our city.
And they brought them, and we put them-- took-- took off their tunics.
Some of them did wear wooden shoes, like Holland shoes, you see.
And we took off, and two more on the same grave.
And after a signal, we started, and the guard go back to the temple there, to the schule, to the--
I was destroyed, emotionally.
You know, I saw the people.
They were like this-- a lot of them were crippled.
A lot of them were-- were old.
A lot of them were probably sick, but I saw a lot of them were young, and you could see bruises on them.
And you could see that they did-- or fight, or that-- where before they got killed, they were really beaten up, or something like that.
See, that's what I saw.
And I saw the tragedy.
I saw something is happening that I didn't expect.
That there is not [INAUDIBLE].
It's not that we-- they're taking us someplace to work in the-- you know, in the eastern part of Poland.
No, no, no.
Because it was from a different area, probably.
And they went-- after that, they took us under guard back to the schule.
And I was looking for my sister and my mother.
I couldn't find them.
What happened-- during the time when I worked there-- I think it was four hours, I worked-- they noticed, the Germans, that there-- still, the train has some empty spaces.
They made another selection.
Additional selection.
And during this selection, my mother and my sister went.
And that's what happened.
I didn't see them anymore.
So--
Treblinka, yes, yeah.
Additionally, you know, they-- [?
Thomas ?] and [?
Geier ?] and the SS made another selection, you know.
I was told later on, because I didn't realize what happened, you see.
I-- What happened, also-- interesting episode.
I tried to-- my friend, my best friend, Mosberg, who's, you know, survived with me, was in school.
He lived just by the temple.
So after my-- my action, they used to call [GERMAN] after action, you see, I wanted to go to them to smuggle myself and to tell them what happened to us, to prepare themselves, you see?
And they-- they caught me to work.
When I came back, I went to them.
And I told them what the situation is.
I smuggled myself through right there, you know, around the schule.
And I told them what to take, and what happens, and so forth, you see.
And I-- when I got from the-- back, I was really very bad, you see.
I couldn't wash.
There was no water.
There was nothing there.
I didn't have any clothes.
Some people gave me some slacks, and they gave me a shirt.
Because it was very hard, you see.
That's what I got at that time.
Except I had my golden watch here, and my-- and a little band around my knuckle.
That's what I had, you see.
Otherwise, I didn't have anything.
And then after that, they made a arbeitslager.
So from about 28,000 people, they left about 1,500 in the-- in the city, out of, that would be 28,000 Jews.
On the 20th, 22nd, 24th.
The 23rd, there was no action, OK?
Oh, they wanted to take a lot of the unnecessary stuff.
And I knew it-- that it would be only a burden for them.
You know, like suitcase, or something like that, you see?
Yes, his parents.
His parents and his sister-- two sisters.
Yes.
Yes, yeah.
And Minsberg, too-- the third one, also.
And Wasser.
We all left.
We always survived.
You see, Wasser worked with me.
Minsberg worked with me.
Mosberg worked with me.
All four of us worked in the factory.
In Henrykow and [?
Ludvikow. ?] Both, yeah.
Originally, we worked in [?
Ludvikow, ?] but they transferred me.
Like-- Minsberg was left in [?
Ludvikow, ?] Mosberg was left in [?
Ludvikow, ?] but Wasser and me went to Henrykow.
That's what happened, you see, there.
Yeah, I'm on my own.
Except I have uncle.
I have uncle and an aunt, and a cousin, and-- a girl cousin, you know, OK?
My uncle was a [INAUDIBLE] it was not chief of police, but he was in charge of an area of police, you know?
Because, you know, he-- that's my father's brother.
And he had a-- a place to live in a-- it was a store front, you know?
And he got this place there.
And that was an arbeitslager, I'm talking.
That's this work camp.
And I was assigned with all the kids to live someplace there, you know, in a group.
And I was in contact with a lot of those girls who were also nurses, you know?
With Natka Koper, who is in Israel, and [?
Renia ?] Kenisburg, lives in Sweden.
And I was very close to them.
And I was maybe a little bit romantically connected with Natka Koper, who is in Israel now.
And my uncle was connected to the Jewish fighting force.
My aunt didn't look at all Jewish.
My cousin, [?
Svi, ?] Henry, didn't either.
They made themselves documents, and they got out from the arbeitslager.
They went under Aryan papers, OK?
So I took over this apartment there of my uncle.
And I got everybody together-- the two girls and Wasser-- Wasser, Mosberg was with somebody else, OK?
And-- but the girl, my cousin, because she looks like-- like, Bradley, but a little darker, you see?
A little bit, you know-- was not blonde.
She went to live with my aunt, who was married to the Pole, who built for them or bought for them this home near a forest.
And what the Polish partisan killed them.
They burned [? them ?] up and, you know.
And I have all the documentation from the court.
I have a transcript of the proceedings of the court, see, by me, you know?
What did they do with all the rings?
What they got and everything, all the valuables.
How they distributed between themselves.
The chief of the partisans got seven years, because he did fight against the Germans and so forth, you see?
So again, I used to live in this arbeitslager.
And again, in columns, we used to go again to work, everyday, and coming back.
And we used to get food over there.
And there was a kitchen also in arbeitslager for soup, or coffee, or something like that, you see?
And this lasted, this kind of work, until 1943.
So '42 from August, OK? 20th to about spring of '43.
Spring '43, they liquidated the arbeitslager.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] OK, the small area where this-- those 1,500 Jews used to live.
Well, they surrounded the place one morning.
Everybody had to go out from the buildings.
You used to sit down on the ground with all hands like this on our-- on our heads, sitting like this.
And they said, who works in this-- hold on, before anything, they counted off some people, you know?
Who works at this factory?
Who works at this factory?
So forth and so on.
I lifted myself up, because I worked here.
Mosberg got up, because worked there, you see?
Wasser, the same.
But Minsberg had an uncle.
And the uncle had something in his house-- something-- some money, or some place there.
So he delayed coming out.
By the time he came, already-- we were already selected to go to the factories.
So Minsberg, one of those four, went to Pionki, to another camp.
So from the 1,500 to Henrykow and [?
Ludvikow ?] went 400 and 400-- 800.
The rest went to Hasag, to Blizyn, and Pionki.
Also in the area, other different camps.
A camp, yeah.
Part of the--
So a factory, and people were attached to the factory, OK?
Pionki was also a factory what they used produce the dynamite, OK?
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] [? pulver. ?]
P-I-O-N-K-I.
Yeah.
And Blizyn was also a camp.
Excuse me.
But they used to have some workplaces to make uniforms, shoes, and so forth for the German army.
Blizyn, B-L-I-Z-- a soft Z-- Y-N.
That was a rigorous camp, Blizyn, OK?
In fact, some people tried to escape from Blizyn to come to us, and from Pionki also to come to us.
Because was-- by us, was a little but not so bad here.
Because I think that the-- the director of the factories, Mr.
Hirsch-- ?] he was an SA member.
That was one of the SS.
But he was not very strict.
And our Henrykow, you see, we had a fellow by the name Fuss, was very, very bad.
F-U-S-S.
Fuss, yeah.
So [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they took us over there, under guard, and we walked.
We walked to [?
Ludvikow ?] originally, OK?
Because Henrykow was not ready yet, the barracks.
Well, I don't-- maybe 4 miles.
That's all.
And when they made a camp for us in Henrykow they transferred me and Wasser to Henrykow Minsberg and Mosberg stayed in [?
Ludvikow, ?] OK?
And Wasser-- we used to have the same bunk, and so forth, you see.
Our barrack mainly was a horse stable with small windows on the top.
It was a big, big, long barrack.
And the roof was made out of tin.
So in-- in summer, it was very, very hot.
In fact, I was assigned-- we had two people to one bunk, me and Wasser.
I was assigned to sleep with him on the bottom.
And fleas were jumping and bite us.
So I gave a piece of bread and so forth, a piece of sausage, I think I gave, and he gave something.
We bribed ourselves to get on the top.
But it was bad, because the sun then hit the roof and was so hot.
But we didn't have a chance, except when we worked night shift.
And we had-- supposed to rest during the day.
It was very, very hot.
And the bed bugs are-- were killing us, too.
You know, a lot of bed bugs there.
And lice.
There was no DDT.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I was-- there was a-- a part for the ladies, for girls, OK?
And part for married people.
And of the policemen, and the chief of the police-- they had a separate area.
And he has a-- a tail going after him.
He has, you know, people who did cooperate with him.
You know, like some kind of a mafia, you know.
And I worked there in Henrykow.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I-- I worked on a-- what we used to make, spokes for wheels.
And wheels.
And the middle part was made in [?
Ludvikow. ?]
And I was not-- there was no protection.
I was cutting once a-- a wood for spoke, and I cut-- amputated my finger here and cut myself here through, you know?
Luckily, that I didn't cut anything.
There was no guard at all, you see.
And I worked also on the outside on the ramp.
We used to unload heavy, redwood, which used to come from Hungary for this-- for the material for the wagons, horse-drawn wagons.
They used to call them Pleskow wagons, the Germans, Pleskow from a town or-- or village in Russia.
And when we worked there, I used to have some of the people-- a lady who used to be a-- a live-in maid by my uncle, by the dentist, used to come to the ramp.
And she went to-- almost to Treblinka by herself, to see.
And she could only go to a small village before Treblinka.
Farther, a Pole even couldn't go there, except those who lived there, you see.
And she came and told me about Treblinka.
She told me that the people are being taken there to the camp, and nobody knows what happens to them, OK?
But a cousin of Wasser, Joseph, Josel-- Josel Wasser, Joseph Wasser-- this size boy.
He was about, maybe 14 or 15 years old.
Very short.
Went to Treblinka.
The people in Treblinka took him and put him in a wagon with the clothes.
Put him in the wagon and the clothes.
And he came.
When he came towards our city, he made a hole in the-- in the wood and jumped out and came to our camp, to arbeitslager.
And told us the story about the station.
Told us a story about this wooden clock, OK?
And about killing machines, and about thousands of people coming daily, and so forth.
Nobody believed him.
The chief of the police [?
Spiegel ?] said not to spread this kind of information, because it's not true.
And we couldn't believe it.
When I discussed the situation with Minsberg, I said, you know what they do with the people?
If it's true that they destroy them, they take the people on a field, and the [?
Stukas ?] come like during the war.
And they kill the people that way.
Because who did imagine that there is something like you see, assembly line, killing people?
That they take-- take thousands of people, in one day, that they can destroy and-- and make ashes out of it?
We didn't believe it.
We couldn't understand it.
Then we came to it.
In the trains, we saw, you know, written in all languages kind, you see, some information in Hebrew, in Jewish, and so forth, from people who were going probably to Treblinka, or something like that.
Some of the wagons, covered wagons.
No, nothing.
Nothing.
Nothing.
No information whatsoever.
Yes, I think that-- yeah.
Because I would expect to get some sign of life.
But we used to speculate, you know.
We used to talk about it when we had time, you know, to find out what.
But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we knew that they will never come back at all, you see?
And so I worked then in the factory in Henrykow.
And I knew that the Germans-- they have a plan for me.
And I knew the situation bleak.
And I said that I will do something.
Even-- I-- I go back a little to the arbeitslager.
In the beginning, people could come in.
My cousin [?
Svi ?] had a cousin.
His aunt, the sister of my aunt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] married a Polish captain who was in charge of the radio station in Wilno, in Poland.
And she got to be Christian.
And through some circumstances, she shot herself and was paralyzed.
But she had a daughter by name of [?
Lydia. ?] And she used to come to the arbeitslager in the beginning and beg me that she can make papers for me.
And I should go, or go to my aunt there where they're by the forest, or go-- And so I said, listen, look at me.
Even I speak perfect Polish with anything, but people will recognize right away that I'm Jewish.
I said, if I go, I go myself, and I will fight.
If I fight, and I get killed, at least I will take somebody with me.
So I knew that the Germans have plans for me that I will not survive.
And I started to plan already to do something.
They selected a group of strong, small boys, but very strong physically.
Everybody knew who was strong, because we did a lot of things-- they are lifting stuff, and the [INAUDIBLE], you see, who was strong, and so forth.
To work in the Gestapo.
The Gestapo was at the other end of the city by the big park and the lagoon in our city.
Our function was to lift up and carry heavy furniture in the Gestapo building.
In the attic, they had a warehouse.
And they collected-- there were no Jews anymore.
They collected things from the Poles, who were all destroyed or sent to Auschwitz, OK?
One day, I go to warehouse where the Poles-- and I moved some coal.
There was a gun laying down.
I took this gun, put it right here in my crotch, you see, attached with the strings, and took it, OK?
And I took it to the-- to the camp.
The Germans did not--
--search us, because they are afraid to get sick.
And it so happened, I took this gun.
And I put it in some grease from a machine, you see, in a little box and buried it in the ground outside the barrack, OK?
Then once, I go on the roof, and there I saw two of officers' boots-- high boots-- open here, cut open here like from a dead body.
So there was, on the attic, they had lime.
A little bit of wood, but mostly was lime.
The attic was made out of lime there on the top.
I took this lime with a little bit of water, spit, and I made them dirty.
Took some strings, tie them around, you see?
And I took them.
Because I used to wear wooden shoes, OK?
Wooden shoes.
It so happened that that day, the Germans decided to put us down in jail temporarily.
I don't know why.
Oh, [INAUDIBLE], they're going to kill us.
They found out that you stole the boot-- I said-- boots.
I said, if they find out I stole, you don't know about anything.
Me-- if they kill me, fine.
They didn't kill us.
They didn't even know about it.
I took the boots, because I prepared myself to escape.
I-- We had a place there of repair, clothes repair, and also shoe repair place.
I gave something to the guy.
He fixed those boots.
I put them away for me, and I was-- I escaped in those boots.
In fact, I escaped-- they were so tight on me that I got blisters, and I couldn't walk after a while.
I had to put my feet in-- in the water.
I almost couldn't take them off.
This is true.
There was a fellow who used to go to our school-- he was a year younger than me-- by the name of [?
Zeneck ?] [?
Zerell-- ?] [?
Zeneck ?] [?
Zerell. ?] And he got a gun.
From where, I don't remember anymore.
He got-- he got a German Luger with seven bullets, OK?
I got a-- news of it.
I said, listen, I have a golden watch with a big chain from my father.
How about exchanging?
He said, sure.
I took the gun.
I gave it to the girl, Natka Koper.
Girlfriend.
She carried it, smuggled it in to the-- to the-- it's, you know, described-- I think it's in the book here, I think, this story also, to some extent.
She took it to the camp.
I went to work.
I put it there in the factory there, in a special-- also grease around, the bullet also-- shouldn't get, you know, rusted.
And I had three guns, because three people were supposed to escape-- me, Wasser, and the nurse.
No, the shoes.
No, the shoes.
I had-- I had-- one I had-- one I had from the coal-- from the coal.
OK?
One I got from [?
Zeneck ?] [?
Zerell, ?] OK?
And one I got-- but I escaped with two of them.
One I got-- it's another story.
A boy escaped from a camp and came to us.
When I cut off my finger, I was on the sick list.
So I didn't work.
They come to me, running, the ladies, from the kitchen.
And the kitchen consisted of two Polish army kitchens, you know, what-- the what do you call this, you know?
On-- On wheels.
With the steel plate, OK?
That was the kitchen, in a tent-- in a tent, outside the barracks.
The [INAUDIBLE] save us.
The boy has a gun.
And if the Gestapo comes here, he is going to kill him.
He said, if you don't do anything, there'll be a calamity.
If he does something, you know, do something.
So I went back to him.
I said, listen, what are you going to do?
Probably want to take you back to the-- to the camp.
Don't make this the-- 400 people here-- jeopardizing, [INAUDIBLE], you see?
He gave me the gun.
I put the gun underneath of the plate, steel plate.
Whatever happened to this, I don't know.
So I had two guns-- actually, two guns.
One had seven bullets, and the other one had three bullets, the-- what I found in the-- in the coal.
And I decided to escape.
And I selected a day, a lucky day-- the 12th of May-- because I finished school at that time.
People watched me like a hawk-- the mafia, even the fellow who is alive, my friend, who brought me the documents to-- to my city to escape, he belonged also to the mafia there, you know?
[INAUDIBLE], you know?
[INAUDIBLE].
He lives in Canada now.
They watched, because they knew that I had family outside, that I'm trying to escape.
But they were afraid to do anything, you see?
It was like-- like United States with Russia, with it's-- with the nuclear bombs, you see?
You know?
So they watched me.
And it so happened that I developed an abscess on my knuckle here.
And I was limping a little bit, and I started to exaggerate.
And I made the date the 12th of May.
And the 12th of May, we decided to escape.
The nurse, not [INAUDIBLE], was not anymore a-- a nurse.
Her girlfriend, [?
Renya ?] Kenisberg who lives now in-- in Sweden, was the nurse.
Because the chief of the plant decided we don't need two nurses.
So when I was transferred, I was working on the night shift, and Wasser also on the night shift.
She was supposed to go out from the barracks and meet us by the latrine, because the wires of the latrine-- you see, the latrines, they jut out a little from the wires.
Because the Poles used to come, then empty outside the-- you know, the residual stays, [INAUDIBLE].
So when I was ready to go, and the Ukrainian was sitting far away with the-- playing with his gun, with his rifle, Wasser run to me.
He said, [INAUDIBLE], they caught Natka.
[INAUDIBLE]-- that was the policeman, a Jewish policeman, caught her, [INAUDIBLE].
She describes this in the book, you know.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] she describes that she went on the-- on the fence, and she got caught on the heel or something like that.
But that isn't-- he caught her because she was not supposed to be there.
She was wearing a-- a-- it was May, and she was wearing a heavy coat, OK?
So he said, what should we do?
I said, we're going.
I will not shoot anybody here.
We have to go.
We have to sacrifice.
Whatever will happen to us will happen.
And we went, OK?
We went.
They caught her.
They took her to the Fuss, to this-- no, first to [?
Spiegel, ?] to the chief of the police.
He beat her up a little bit.
Then he took her to Fuss.
Fuss started to beat her, find out, where did we go?
She didn't know.
I didn't know myself.
My plans were to go to, maybe make contact with this-- with the Kosubec, who was a partisan.
But originally, I wanted to go maybe east, to more [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] areas which are more left [? leaning, ?] you see?
Where there were some people who were more pro-Semitic, not anti-Semitic like in my area.
Because my area was very, very anti-Semitic.
Kielce was a known place of anti-Semitism.
So she didn't know.
So they beat her up, beat her up.
Finally, the [?
Spiegel ?] could not take the responsibility.
He asked her, Natka, what would happen?
Would you try to escape again?
She said, yes.
If I will have a chance, I will escape.
So he gave her to the Gestapo, the Gestapo sent her to Auschwitz.
Luckily in Auschwitz, she met old prisoners who had some privileges.
And they helped her out.
She survived.
Besides that, she was a political prisoner.
They didn't cut her hair.
She wore the red triangle as a prisoner, because if somebody was a political prisoner in-- in Auschwitz, they-- they didn't kill.
Because if Gestapo wanted to find out some more from them, they'd have to keep them alive.
And that's why she survived.
